title: About the transition from the Household Economic Survey to the Household Income and Living Survey | Stats NZ
content:
## About the transition from the Household Economic Survey to the Household Income and Living Survey
12 February 2026, 2:00pm
This report introduces the Household Income and Living Survey (HILS) and outlines key differences with the Household Economic Survey (HES).
HILS replaces HES from the 2024/2025 year onwards. HILS was introduced to streamline and modernise how we collect and produce household income, expenditure, net worth, and child poverty statistics. It incorporates updated collection technology, questionnaire design, and sample design to reduce the survey burden on respondents, improve data quality, and ensure the ongoing sustainability of producing high-quality statistics for Aotearoa New Zealand.
Household income, housing costs, and child poverty statistics from HILS for the year ended June 2025 will be published on 26 February 2026.
Key differences between the two surveys for users to be aware of relate to the collection of material wellbeing data and measurement of material hardship; the questions used to derive disability status; and which properties are in scope for the collection of expenditure on housing costs.
At a high level, these design changes mean that:
- researchers can access more comprehensive material wellbeing data within households
- disability statistics better represent disabled populations
- housing costs more closely align with the concept of the essential costs of shelter.
The changes also mean that we have updated the methodology used for deriving material hardship rates.
Measuring child poverty: Material hardship in the Household Income and Living Survey provides further information about this methodology update.
Published statistics on household income, housing costs, and child poverty produced from HILS can be compared with those produced from HES, with one main exception. Material hardship rates by disability status for children should not be compared between the two surveys, due to the combination of changes to the disability questions and the measurement of material hardship. A new time series will begin from the year ended June 2025.
Users of housing costs data should note that while there is no major impact to high-level statistics published by Stats NZ, the change to how housing costs are collected and derived may have an impact on comparability between HES and HILS for specific, detailed breakdowns. Nevertheless, HES datasets include detailed housing costs expenditure which can be used by researchers to derive housing costs variables that allow for direct comparability between the surveys. We can support researchers with code and instructions on how to do this. Contact details are under Technical enquiries , at the end of this report.
The primary objective of both HES and HILS is to facilitate the analysis and monitoring of the social and economic welfare of New Zealand. Social and economic welfare is measured through income, expenditure, housing costs, net worth, and non-monetary measures.
HILS will be used to report on child poverty as defined by the Child Poverty Reduction Act 2018 (the Act).
The purpose of the Act is “to help achieve a significant and sustained reduction in child poverty in New Zealand” by setting provisions that encourage a focus on child poverty reduction, facilitate political accountability, and require transparency in reporting. To achieve its purpose, the Act specifies child poverty measures, and requires the setting of reduction targets and related reporting. HES has historically provided estimates for reporting on nine of the 10 child poverty measures specified by the Act, excluding persistent child poverty.
In 2022, Stats NZ implemented a longitudinal survey, Living in Aotearoa, to enable reporting on all 10 child poverty measures. A longitudinal survey collects information from the same respondent cohorts over time and therefore Living in Aotearoa allowed for the measurement of persistent child poverty. As part of the development of this survey, Stats NZ took the opportunity to review several elements of the HES design and incorporate improvements and efficiencies.
In early 2024, the Government Statistician made the decision to discontinue Living in Aotearoa, due to unsustainable data collection and the ongoing cost profile of the survey. Instead, HILS, a cross-sectional survey, was established to replace HES from July 2024.
HILS incorporates many of the questionnaire design updates that were made for Living in Aotearoa. The first HILS collection started in July 2024. Stats NZ will publish the first child poverty and household income and housing costs statistics from HILS in February 2026.

## HILS methodology and design

### Survey and sample design
Like HES, HILS is an annual survey that runs from July to June each year. The survey measures household income and housing costs every year and collects additional wealth and expenditure data from a smaller sample every three years. The survey objectives and target population remain the same, as do the key variables and outputs, use of administrative data, and much of the methodology for transforming survey responses into final estimates.
HILS is a sample survey, which means that rather than interviewing every household in New Zealand, a smaller number of households are selected from across the country. Each of these households helps to represent different people and communities around the country.
The sample is designed to produce official statistics that are representative of New Zealand and key population groups, meet the requirements of the Act, and allow for the ongoing sustainability of producing high-quality statistics.
HILS is designed to achieve responses from a representative sample of 17,000 households. The sample is stratified to ensure representation from different subgroups, including low-income households and regions across the country, and includes a booster sample to increase the number of Māori sampled.
Detailed information about the HILS design and methodology will be available on 26 February 2026 in Child poverty statistics: Year ended June 2025 – technical appendix .

### Measuring persistent child poverty
The Act requires Stats NZ to report on poverty persistence.
Stats NZ has developed a methodology for reporting on persistent child poverty using an approach that combines survey data from HES and HILS with administrative data (data collected from other sources and by other agencies). This approach allows poverty circumstances to be understood over time.
Developing a methodology to measure persistent child poverty using survey and admin data explains how Stats NZ will measure persistent poverty without the Living in Aotearoa survey.

### Introduction of the proxy module
A ‘proxy’ response refers to a situation where someone answers survey questions on behalf of a household member who cannot be interviewed themselves but whose affairs they know well. HILS introduces a proxy module as part of the survey questionnaire to allow Stats NZ to collect information from individuals who may not otherwise be able to respond, providing more complete household income and demographic data.
The purpose of this module is not to collect comprehensive information about proxied individuals, but to collect core information that allows the best chance at linking individuals to administrative data in the Integrated Data Infrastructure (IDI). Integrated Data Infrastructure has more information about the IDI.
Linking to administrative data allows us to directly collect income from wages and salaries and government transfers. The proxy module therefore focuses on key demographic and labour status questions including full name, age/date of birth, gender, ethnicity, work type, employment arrangement in the last seven days, and retirement status. Identifying information is then removed from the final datasets used for statistical analysis.
Proxies are only able to be completed with the informed consent of the individual being proxied for and can be refused.

## Summary of key differences and impacts to continuity
HILS includes design elements to improve data quality, reduce respondent burden, and ensure the ongoing sustainability of producing high-quality statistics, while remaining broadly comparable with statistics produced from HES. Many of these changes were first developed for Living in Aotearoa and carried over for HILS. Nevertheless, there are some key differences with HES for users to be aware of.
The most significant areas of change are in:
- the collection of material wellbeing data and measurement of material hardship
- the questions used to derive disability status
- which properties are in scope for the collection and calculation of expenditure on housing costs.
Stats NZ undertook a detailed assessment of the impact of these design changes to ensure that statistics produced by HILS are of sufficient quality and fit for the purpose of meeting legislative requirements and customer needs. Part of this involved assessing the continuity of statistics between the HES-based series concluding in 2023/2024 and the HILS-based series commencing 2024/2025, and identifying areas that are not appropriate for comparison. Depending on the design change, continuity was assessed using datasets from one or more of HES, HILS, and Living in Aotearoa.
The assessment showed that key statistics from HILS may be used for the same purposes as those from HES, and that in nearly all cases, these can be directly compared between the two surveys in a continuous time series. Users of more granular data than Stats NZ publishes do, however, need to be aware of the changes, as comparability might be affected for certain use cases.
Exceptions to this are material hardship-based statistics for children by disability status. The estimates for hardship by disability status from the 2024/2025 HILS onwards cannot be compared to previous estimates from HES (discussed further in Material hardship by disability status below). The series impacted are tables 3.04, 8.04, and 9.04 in Child poverty statistics: Year ended June 2025 , to be published on 26 February 2026.
It is recommended that data users familiarise themselves with the differences between HES and HILS, and exercise caution when working with or interpreting data that has undergone any amount of change.
The key differences are described in more detail in the sections below.

## Material hardship by disability status
Material hardship in both HES and HILS is a non-income-based measure of poverty. Non-income measures provide a direct indication of the actual day-to-day living conditions of households such as access to the basics of food, clothing, accommodation, heating, and transport, and their ability to afford other items that most people would regard as essential or near-essential.
Disability status in both HES and HILS identifies people who have long-term difficulties in functional areas such as seeing, hearing, walking, cognition, self-care, and communication. The functional areas vary depending on life stage.
In HILS, changes have been made both to the methodology used to measure material hardship and the design of questions about disability . While the material hardship methodology update allowed for a continuous series of estimates for children overall and for most population groups, this is not the case for series relating to material hardship by disability status.
The change in hardship rates for disabled children and children living in households with one or more disabled people (referred to from here as ‘disabled households’) between HES and HILS reflects a combination of real-world change over time and design change. Compared with other demographic groups over the same period, the combined changes to disability and material hardship measurement have led to a stronger impact on hardship rates.
The magnitude of the impact on material hardship rates among disabled children and children in disabled households means that statistics for the year ended June 2025 cannot be compared to previous years. The series impacted are tables 3.04, 8.04, and 9.04 in Child poverty statistics: Year ended June 2025 .
There is evidence of impact on income poverty measures for disabled children. The impact is not of sufficient magnitude to warrant a break in time series, but we do advise caution when comparing HES to HILS.
Other statistics such as household incomes and housing costs by disability status are comparable over time, as are material hardship statistics for other demographic groups.
The changes to each of these concepts are described in more detail in the following sections.

## Material wellbeing questions and material hardship

### Changes to the material wellbeing questions
Changes have been made to the design of the material wellbeing questions in HILS, including to questionnaire structure, respondent selection and eligibility, response options, and questionnaire wording. These changes were introduced to improve clarity and comprehension for respondents, improve accuracy of responses and reduce non-response, and update wording to maintain real-world relevance.
A key improvement is that everyone aged 15+ in the household now receives questions about their personal material wellbeing, and a nominated ‘best person to answer financial questions on behalf of the household’ also answers household-level material wellbeing questions. In HES, a randomly selected person aged 18+ was selected to complete all material wellbeing questions – both personal and household-level.
This update comprises an improvement to the overall accuracy of household-level responses and allows users of HILS datasets to examine intra-household material wellbeing – something that was not possible using HES data.
Measuring child poverty: Material hardship in the Household Income and Living Survey provides further information on the material wellbeing design differences between surveys.

### Updates to the material hardship methodology
Material hardship in both HES and HILS is a non-income-based measure of poverty. Non-income measures provide a direct indication of the actual day-to-day living conditions of households such as access to the basics of food, clothing, accommodation, heating, and transport, and their ability to afford other items that most people would regard as essential or near-essential.
Both HES and HILS contain sets of questions relating to material wellbeing, a subset of which are used to create an index for reporting on material hardship. In HES, Stats NZ used the ‘DEP-17’ index to measure material hardship, a deprivation index developed by the Ministry for Social Development for New Zealand.
As a result of the changes made to the material wellbeing questions and their observed impact on the data, Stats NZ updated the methodology for measuring material hardship. For HES, a household was determined to be in material hardship if it was deprived of six or more items (out of 17) of the DEP-17 index. For HILS, material hardship is calculated using an updated index called the ‘MH-18’, with a threshold of seven or more out of 18 items.
The update to the material hardship methodology was implemented to ensure consistency in the hardship concept being measured and continuity between HILS-based rates of material hardship and the HES-based time series.
Overall, material hardship statistics from HILS may be used for the same purposes as those from HES. However, it is advised that material hardship rates by disability status for children should not be compared between the two surveys. This means that the estimates from the 2024/2025 HILS onwards cannot be compared to previous estimates. The series impacted are tables 3.04, 8.04, and 9.04 in Child poverty statistics: Year ended June 2025 .
Measuring child poverty: Material hardship in the Household Income and Living Survey provides more information about the updated material hardship methodology.

## Disability question changes

### Changes to the disability questions
Stats NZ uses the Washington Group on Disability Statistics (WG) question sets in its household surveys and in the census. The WG focuses on the development of disability measures for use in surveys to provide internationally comparable measures of disability. The questions focus on identifying people who have long-term difficulties in functional areas such as seeing, hearing, walking, cognition, self-care, and communication. The functional areas differ slightly depending on the life stage of the person being interviewed, and the child questions are designed to be asked of a nominated adult.
The WG has developed several different question sets depending on the length and level of detail required (see Question Sets – the Washington Group on Disability Statistics ). Both HES and HILS use modified versions of the Washington Group Short Set on Functioning Enhanced (WG-SS Enhanced) and the Washington Group / UNICEF Child Functioning Module (CFM).
While HILS is not designed to report on disability rates or produce detailed disability statistics (as is the Household Disability Survey (HDS)), use of the enhanced short sets allows for reporting on child poverty measures for disabled children and children in disabled households, as required by the Act. It also allows for reporting on household income and housing costs statistics for all disabled people and disabled households.
Changes to the disability questions were first recommended for Living in Aotearoa, and these recommendations informed HILS. The changes relate to the questions provided to people aged 15+ and the questions about 2 to 14-year-olds.
At a high level, the changes made to the disability questions are that:
- 15 to 17-year-olds are now asked the adult set of questions. This aligns with how the questions are asked in the HDS, and with advice received from the WG.
- improvements have been made to disability question wording across the survey. The questions now clarify that they are about long-term health issues or conditions (with ‘long-term’ defined as longer than six months). Further, the questions reflect updates made to WG module documentation and feedback from cognitive testing.
Specific changes to the question design and wording include:
- splitting alternative questions: in HILS, questions that contain more than one functional criterion are now split into separate items. For example, “do you have difficulty remembering or concentrating?” was divided into one question about remembering only and one question about concentrating only.
- adding clarifying text: in addition to defining “long-term difficulty” in the module introductions, clarifying text is now incorporated throughout the question set to better communicate the intended scope of the question. This included: specifying the absence of aids or assistance (eg, “ Without using equipment or assistance , how much difficulty do you have walking?”) specifying the source of difficulty (eg, “ Because of a long-term condition or health problem , do you have difficulty concentrating?”) providing more concrete examples (eg, “ Personal care includes things like… ”) clarifying terminology (eg, specifying that “feeling depressed” refers to low mood, not clinical depression) defining the comparative group for child items (eg, “ Compared with other children of the same age , how much difficulty does [your child] have…”).
- specifying the absence of aids or assistance (eg, “ Without using equipment or assistance , how much difficulty do you have walking?”)
- specifying the source of difficulty (eg, “ Because of a long-term condition or health problem , do you have difficulty concentrating?”)
- providing more concrete examples (eg, “ Personal care includes things like… ”)
- clarifying terminology (eg, specifying that “feeling depressed” refers to low mood, not clinical depression)
- defining the comparative group for child items (eg, “ Compared with other children of the same age , how much difficulty does [your child] have…”).
The changes were made in order to bring HILS into better alignment with the HDS, improve conceptual alignment and respondent experience, and maintain best practice in questionnaire design. They are considered improvements that allow Stats NZ to produce more accurate statistics about disabled people and disabled households.

### Assessment of impacts
A detailed assessment was undertaken to understand the impact on the measurement and identification of the disabled population, and the responses provided by that population to the survey. This included examining individual question responses, the demographic distribution of the disabled population, the disability rates for demographic groups, and statistics produced for the disabled population for child poverty, household income, and housing costs.
The largest impact observed was to the individual question responses and resulting disability rate of 15 to 17-year-olds. This was expected, as they received a different question set than they had in the past. One key area of change is in the way anxiety is asked about; the child set only asks about frequency of feeling, rather than frequency and intensity, as in the adult set.
Overall, 15 to 17-year-olds are a small proportion of the total person and 15+ populations. These changes have therefore had minimal impact to the demographic composition of these populations (in terms of ethnicity, age, etc) and there is no evidence of an impact to the incomes and housing costs reported in HILS. (Note also that the disability rates produced by HILS are now much closer to the rates produced by the 2023 HDS.)
However, there has been a change to the age composition of disabled children (aged 2 to 17), with the ratio of 2 to 14-year-olds to 15 to 17-year-olds shifting. Statistics released from HILS for this group will reflect this change in composition. This shift has varying degrees of impact on key statistics depending on the nature of the relationships between age, disability status, and outcome variable.
There is evidence of impact on income poverty measures for disabled children. The impact is not of sufficient magnitude to warrant a break in time series, but we do advise caution when comparing HES to HILS.
When combined with the changes to the measurement of material hardship, however, the magnitude of the impact on material hardship rates among disabled children and children in disabled households means that statistics for the year ended June 2025 cannot be compared to previous years.
Statistics by disability status may broadly be used for the same purposes as HES, except for child poverty measures involving material hardship – for these, there has been a break in the time series. The series impacted are tables 3.04, 8.04, and 9.04 in Child poverty statistics: Year ended June 2025 .

## Housing costs changes

### Housing costs statistics
Both HES and HILS were designed to produce statistics on housing costs. ‘Housing costs’ consist of expenditure on mortgage principal repayments, mortgage interest payments, mortgage application fees, rent payments, other payments associated with renting (eg, bonds paid in the last 12 months), property rates payments (both regional and local government), and payments associated with building-related insurance.
Stats NZ uses housing costs to measure household income after deducting housing costs (AHC). AHC income is then used to estimate child poverty for the following measures under the Act:
b) Low income: less than 50% median equivalised disposable household income after deducting housing costs for the base financial year
f) Low income: less than 60% median equivalised disposable household income after deducting housing costs for the financial year
g) Low income: less than 50% median equivalised disposable household income after deducting housing costs for the financial year
h) Low income: less than 40% median equivalised disposable household income after deducting housing costs for the financial year
j) Low income and hardship: less than 60% median equivalised disposable household income after deducting housing costs for the financial year and material hardship.

### Changes between surveys
In HILS, expenditure on housing costs is asked only for the main property, whereas HES asked about additional properties owned by the respondent.
While HES was designed to measure all expenditure on housing, it was determined that for poverty measurement in HILS, expenditure on other properties was not needed to measure the costs of ‘providing shelter,’ which is the conceptual focus.
Removing questions about additional properties reduces the length of the survey in households with complex arrangements, reducing respondent burden. Expenditure across all properties will continue to be collected every three years when detailed household expenditure is collected from a sub-sample of the HILS survey. The next detailed expenditure year release will be for the year ended June 2026.
A detailed assessment was undertaken to assess the impact of excluding additional properties and to determine whether it was necessary to adjust previously released estimates to preserve the time series. The HES datasets captured detailed housing costs information and allowed for expenditure on additional properties to be separated from the main property. We therefore compared the impact on published HES estimates before and after additional property is incorporated.
Across 2018/2019 through to 2023/2024, 4 percent to 7 percent of households had a difference in housing costs before and after excluding additional property.
The assessment showed only small differences on AHC child poverty measures for the total population, all of which were within sample error and did not move the poverty rates in a consistent direction. The average difference for measure (b) was 0.3 percentage points, 0.01 percentage points for measures (f), (g), and (j), and 0.2 percentage points for measure (h).
There was also minimal change to published AHC income medians and means across regions; most of the impact was observed when looking directly at housing costs. For all households, median and mean housing costs estimates reduced in each year by about $500, although the trends over time were unchanged and the shape of the distributions remained stable.
As such, while the change in methodology has had a small impact on specific published estimates, data users may continue to use the estimates for the same purposes as HES.
While there is no major impact to high-level statistics published by Stats NZ, the change to how housing costs are collected and derived may have an impact on comparability between HES and HILS for specific, detailed breakdowns. Nevertheless, HES datasets allow additional property costs to be isolated, allowing researchers to derive housing costs variables that allow for direct comparability between the surveys. We can support researchers with code and instructions on how to do this. Contact details are under Technical enquiries below.
